techniques.evidence_quote: a former contractor accessed one of Chegg's S3 databases using an AWS Root Credential
Chegg shared the AWS Root Credentials among its employees and even outside contractors
exfiltrated a database containing personal information of approximately 40 million users
the former contractor exfiltrated a database containing personal information of approximately 40 million users of the Chegg platform

attack_chain.description: A former contractor used a shared AWS Root Credential (which had not been rotated after their departure) to authenticate to Chegg's AWS environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: In or around April 2018, a former contractor accessed one of Chegg's S3 databases using an AWS Root Credential.

attack_chain.description: Using the root credential's full administrative privileges, the contractor accessed an S3 bucket containing personal information of approximately 40 million users.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Using the AWS Root Credentials, the former contractor exfiltrated a database containing personal information of approximately 40 million users

evidence_chunks.quote: Using the AWS Root Credentials, the former contractor exfiltrated a database containing personal information of approximately 40 million users
Using the AWS Root Credentials, the former contractor exfiltrated a database containing personal information of approximately 40 million users

attack_chain.description: The contractor exfiltrated the S3 user database containing names, email addresses, passwords (MD5-hashed), dates of birth, and Scholarship Search Data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the former contractor exfiltrated a database containing personal information of approximately 40 million users of the Chegg platform

evidence_chunks.quote: the former contractor exfiltrated a database containing personal information of approximately 40 million users of the Chegg platform
the former contractor exfiltrated a database containing personal information of approximately 40 million users of the Chegg platform

attack_chain.description: Threat actors later cracked the weak MD5 password hashes; approximately 25 million plaintext passwords appeared in an online forum in September 2018.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: approximately 25 million of the exfiltrated passwords in plain text, meaning the threat actors had cracked the hash for those passwords

evidence_chunks.quote: approximately 25 million of the exfiltrated passwords in plain text, meaning the threat actors had cracked the hash for those passwords
approximately 25 million of the exfiltrated passwords in plain text, meaning the threat actors had cracked the hash for those passwords

initial_access_vector: Former contractor reused a shared AWS Root Credential that provided full administrative access to S3; credentials were never rotated after contractor departure and MFA was not required.
persistence_mechanism: Not required - shared, non-rotated AWS root access key continued to work after the contractor's engagement ended.
privilege_escalation: None needed - the shared credential was the AWS root user with full administrative privileges over all S3 data.
